text: The following video explains and demonstrates the use of the Da Vinci platform to perform robotic abdominal bone length fixation. The picture is a female bone basin wall. From the left to the right from the tip of the sword to the bone basin base. The weak structure of the bone basin base will cause the upper trachea to slacken and the muscle of the bone basin base will loosen. When the pressure inside the abdomen increases, the bone basin base will move downward.
This surgery will be completed by a surgeon using a V-shaped machine arm.

Compared to traditional opening and closing surgery, micro-mechanical arm suture operation is easy and has micro-creating advantages, and post-surgical recovery is better. What you see now is an X-ray photo of a female patient who does not have a permanent protrusion and is having difficulty in defecation. Because the muscles of the bone basin are loose, the bone basin moves downward every time the defecation is used, and when it is relaxed, it returns to its original position, causing difficulty in defecation.

The left side of the arrowhead is the most conspicuous area of the retina. The right side of the arrowhead is the mucous membrane. You can see the mucous membrane. This type of patient may also have the symptoms of analgesia or discoloration. Sometimes four symptoms will appear at the same time. These symptoms will cause dysfunctions.

The surgeon uses an adjuvant to establish an entrance and remove abdominal adhesions in a female patient with a soft-tongue cystic ovarian opening. Adhesions are temporarily fixed on the left abdomen wall, while the cyst is temporarily fixed on the right abdomen wall. A Two-O-P-D sewing line is used to create a bow with a fishing line. The camera and three robotic arms provide a view of the deep pelvic basin.

The surgeon creates four eight-centimeter openings in the abdominal cavity for the insertion of robotic arms and instruments. The first insertion is made, followed by two additional openings on the right side. Surgical nails and scissors are introduced through these ports, with the left nail being adjusted. A fifth port is utilized by the assistant to aid in the procedure.

The surgeon carefully cuts the oblique muscles located above the buttocks, taking care to avoid the main nerves responsible for sexual and reproductive functions. The incision is then extended along the right side of the rectum.

The surgeon performs a ventral mesh rectopexy, entering from the anterior aspect of the rectum. The robotic system provides a three-dimensional view with magnification, allowing precise visualization of the surgical field. The camera pans left and right to show the sound-to-speech interval marked by an arrow, where cutting is performed. The dissection continues until the muscles at the bottom of the pelvic cavity are exposed. The mesh is sewn onto the muscles on both sides of the pelvic wall and the anterior rectal wall to stabilize the structure. The bone marrow wall is visible at the bottom left of the screen, shrinking upon activation of the electronic device. The rectum's position is identified either by cleaning or by the assistant inserting a finger to pinpoint the correct area, confirming the cut has reached the bottom of the pelvic rectum.

The surgeon inserts the mesh film into the surgical field, using robotic arms and tweezers to position it correctly. The widest part of the mesh is placed at the bottom of the pelvic cavity. The surgeon then inserts the OPDES sewing needle and begins sewing the first stitch on the muscle at the bottom of the pelvic cavity on the left side, ensuring the PDS suture line can be easily broken if needed.

The assistant removes the broken sewing line after the completion of the sewing. The surgeon places the third stitch superficially on the philtrum, ensuring it does not penetrate deeply to avoid entering the rectum and causing infection. A prosthetic incisor is used to confirm that the suture has not penetrated the rectum.

The surgeon removes the previous sewing line and needle head, replacing them with a new sewing line and needle head. The surgeon then sews the right side of the muscle at the bottom of the pelvic basin, using deep stitches to ensure the strength and stability of the pelvic basin. The arrow indicates the position of the muscle at the bottom of the basin, which is pressed to confirm its location.

The surgeon removes the sewing needle and thread from the hand after completing the sewing. The third needle is inserted on the left side of the bone basin, and the webbing is pulled up towards the bottom of the bone. Using a non-dissoluble One Exhibit sewing line, the webbing is sewn on top of the bottom of the bone, which protrudes in the middle of the body. The stitch is sewn deeply to ensure the webbing remains secure.

The video shows the seam line being broken, highlighting the ease of breaking compared to using a PDS seam line. The arrow indicates the lower bone, situated between two large blood vessels. The surgeon carefully confirms the location of the lower bone to avoid damaging the blood vessels and causing significant bleeding.

The surgeon uses scissors to assist with the SC-Boot sewing line, which is sewn on the right side of the bottom bone clip. The arrow indicates the proximity of the sewing line to the urethra, which is visually close, approximately two centimeters away.

The surgeon cuts off the seam line with scissors and removes excess mesh lining by hand. A Two-O PDS sewing line is inserted to sew the fat around the left side of the rectum, ensuring direct contact between the rectum and surrounding tissue to enhance resilience. The arrow indicates the location of three main nerves related to sexual function and fertility.

The surgeon inserts a V-Lock three-o-p-d-s sewing needle to close the mesh lining at the top. The mesh is currently tight but will loosen over time. The sewing line features a small loop, allowing movement in one direction without knot tying. The surgeon carefully passes the sewing line through the small hole at the end, ensuring the seam is closed to prevent direct contact with the mesh lining. The seam allowance is picked up, particularly on the right side, with caution to avoid opening the needle.

The surgeon completes the closure of the mesh lining and cuts the sewing line, removing it from the surgical field. The joint of the chair fixed to the left side wall is opened, returning it to its original position. The same procedure is performed on the right side of the abdominal wall, releasing the soft tissue and restoring it to its original position.

The surgeon removes the sewing line that temporarily held the uterus in place, followed by the removal of all surgical equipment from the abdominal cavity. The DaVinci robotic platform is used for abdomen fillet rectal fixation.
